name: Chris Sale
reasoning: Recent reporting flatly contradicts the stats-only model's inputs: Sale's last start was 89 pitches over seven scoreless innings against Texas with six strikeouts, and he's carrying a 2.06 ERA with 10.5 K/9 across 18 starts this season, not a 3.6 IP/start workload or 0 days rest — those figures look stale, likely reflecting an earlier ribcage-injury-shortened stretch rather than his current workhorse form. He's also historically dominant versus San Diego specifically, going 4-0 with a 1.80 ERA in six career starts against the Padres and riding a 10-inning scoreless streak into this one, which supports a strikeout total well above the model's 4.3 projection. The Padres' bats (Tatis, Machado, France) are hot since the All-Star break, which could push balls in play rather than whiffs, but given Sale's normal turn through the rotation and recent 6-K/7-IP outing, a total near or slightly above the 6.5 market line looks more defensible than the stats-only model's low projection.

name: Randy Dobnak
reasoning: Dobnak's underlying 2026 numbers are far weaker than the trailing-K/9 snapshot suggests: per one recent preview his season strikeout rate sits at just 11.3% with a 12.9% walk rate and 5.60 xERA, and his primary sinker (43% usage, ~92 mph) is generating only a 6.3% whiff rate. He's also been on a short leash all year, throwing about 8.3 fewer pitches per outing than league average, and in his lone MLB start this season he needed 96 pitches to get just 13 outs (4.1 IP) while striking out only 3 against San Diego. That combination of a contact-oriented sinker/changeup arsenal, low whiff rates, and quick hooks makes the model's 3.7 projection look optimistic; I'd shade this down closer to 3.0, below both the model and the 3.5 market line, especially against a Tigers lineup with patient left-handed bats (Riley Greene, McGonigle) near the top of the order.

name: Taj Bradley
reasoning: Bradley owns a dominant track record vs Cleveland specifically — a 1.54 ERA over four career starts with 31 strikeouts in 23.1 innings, including a 10-K, seven-inning gem against this same Guardians lineup just over two weeks ago where he leaned heavily on a cutter that generated 17 whiffs. That supports the stats-only model's 22.1% opponent K-rate input and its 6.2 projection, arguably pushing it slightly higher given the matchup-specific history. The one caution is his most recent outing (July 18 at the Cubs) was rough — 5 IP, 5 ER, 6 K, 3 BB, snapping a three-start win streak — which tempers workload expectations slightly, though the K rate in that shortened outing (6 in 5 IP) was still in line with his season 27.2% K rate. On the lineup side, Jose Ramirez's activation after a 5-week hamate fracture injury adds a quality bat back, but manager Stephen Vogt has explicitly flagged 'limitations' and a cautious plan for his workload, and Cleveland's offense minus Ramirez ranked 28th in the majors in runs, so the return doesn't materially change the strikeout-prone profile this lineup showed in Bradley's last start against them.

reasoning: Beat coverage frames Pfaadt as having 'turned a corner' since his June 30 recall, with reporters noting he is 'attacking hitters early' and pitching efficiently, but explicitly note he 'hasn't consistently worked deep into games, averaging around five innings' -- consistent with the model's 5.2 IP/start input and capping his strikeout ceiling. However, a same-day preview flags a real red flag: Pfaadt 'carries a 4.74 ERA and has allowed a .440 slugging percentage' on the road, suggesting more hard, early contact away from Chase Field, which could mean quicker/shorter outings against a Cardinals lineup that features productive bats in Jordan Walker (.289/22 HR/76 RBI) and Alec Burleson. There's no injury or workload-limitation news, and rest (5 days) is normal, so I'm not deviating sharply from the model's 3.9 projection, but the road-split contact concern nudges the projection down slightly to just under the 3.9 baseline, still comfortably above the 3.5 market line.
